items.text: • had to water as soon as i got home, it was 96 and climbing (102 max)

items.title: Terminal Tools
items.text:
• tldr - The tldr-pages project is a collection of community-maintained help pages for command-line tools, that aims to be a simpler, more approachable complement to traditional man pages. There is also a web based app called tldr InBrowser.App

  • duf - Disk Usage/Free Utility.

  • zoxide - is a smarter cd command for your terminal. It keeps track of the

     directories you use most frequently, and uses a ranking algorithm to
 navigate to the best match.

  • fzf - (Fuzzy Finder) is a lightning-fast, interactive command-line utility used to search and filter any list of items (files, command history, processes, git branches).

  • bat - cat with wings

  • gh - is GitHub on the command line. It brings pull requests, issues, and other GitHub concepts to the terminal next to where you are already working with git and your code.

  • htop - is an interactive, real-time process viewer and system monitor for Unix-like operating systems. It serves as a user-friendly, feature-rich alternative to the traditional top command, allowing you to easily manage system resources, monitor CPU/Memory usage, and kill or renice processes without needing their exact PIDs.

  • less - The less command in Linux is used to view the contents of a file one page at a time without opening it in an editor, making it ideal for reading large files efficiently.

  • pandoc - Pandoc is a Haskell library for converting from one markup format to another, and a command-line tool that uses this library.

  • ripgrep - is a line-oriented search tool that recursively searches the current directory for a regex pattern.

  • sed - GNU stream editor for filtering and transforming text.

  • tmux

  • EZA - s a modern alternative for the venerable file-listing command-line program ls that ships with Unix and Linux operating systems, giving it more features and better defaults. It uses colours to distinguish file types and metadata. It knows about symlinks, extended attributes, and Git.

  • calc - is an interactive calculator which provides for easy large numeric calculations, but which also can be easily programmed for difficult or long calculations. It can accept a command line argument, in which case it executes that single command and exits.

  • atuin = replaces your existing shell history with a SQLite database, and records additional context for your commands. Additionally, it provides optional and fully encrypted synchronisation of your history between machines, via an Atuin server.

  • yazi - is a terminal file manager written in Rust, based on non-blocking async I/O. It aims to provide an efficient, user-friendly, and customizable file management experience.

  • croc - is a tool that allows any two computers to simply and securely transfer files and folders.

items.title: Install NGINX
items.text:
In order to have a web server in production mode the approach would be different (find more information on the official website of NGINX)but just for testing purposes:

$ sudo apt install nginx

It will ask for confirmation, then start installing the package. After that we can confirm the installation with:

$ sudo nginx -v

You will get the version of the NGINX installed. Then we can start the service with:

$ sudo systemctl start nginx.service

And check the service is running with:

$ systemctl status nginx

You are done if you see “active (running)”! Test with cURL or a browser with the server public IP:

$ curl http://<public_ip>

• Finally got my OCI server to display a nginx webserver example page

  • Issue was i needed to open port 80 using iptables, not just VSN on OCI dashboard

To allow port 80 to access my site I had to define a new rule in the iptables rules file on the server. Navigate to /etc/iptables/rules.v4 and add a new line;

-A INPUT -p tcp -m state --state NEW -m tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT

• Working on my personal blog

  • Using MarkText to edit the .md files https://github.com/marktext/marktext

  • Using Zensical to create the static site https://github.com/zensical/zensical

  • Planning on adding site to my OCI server

• Used uv run zensical build on the directory of .md files for my blog

  • Moved the generated files in the automatically created site directory to the server using croc send on the client, and sudo croc on the server

  • I moved the whole site directory over to the server in ~/var/www/html/site

  • I added the /site to the default root location in nginx by moding a server rule found in /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/default

server { listen 80 default_server; listen [[::]]:80 default_server;

root /var/www/html/site;

index index.html index.htm index.nginx-debian.html;


server_name _;
  • This will make it easier to rebuild the website after deleting the old zensical build site dir (from client and server). Then running uv zensical build and moving that newly created directory to the server.

• Moved this blog from a OCI server (an learning experiment) to Github Pages today, seems much easier to make edits, rebuild the site with Zensical build and then push to my repo for automatic rendering.

• Working on note taking work-flow.
- open terminal
- type vn (for a nvim doc openned in the Github/notes directory
- add content
- save file as a .md file
- run$ git status to see any changes that need commits
- run$ git add [dir/file]
- run$ git status to make sure correct file is staged
- at terminal prompt run$ git commit -m "Add New Note"
- then $ git push
  • I am using a modified version of this flow to work on this blog

items.title: How I Edit this blog
items.text:
• After creating any edits to this content and saving:

  • I open a terminal and using rm site -r , I delete the Site directory from the Zensical Ja_blog directory

  • Then I run uv run zensical build to rebuild the sites html

  • I then move any new or changed files/directories to the Documents/Github/AllPilsnersRLagers.github.io dir

  • cd into that directory and run git status and the other git steps outlined above
